FLOOR AND CEILING — Fundamentals put floor (production cost) and ceiling (substitution cost) under commodities. Below floor → supply shrinks → price rises. Above ceiling → demand switches. Same price areas serve as floor year after year. For crypto: mining cost = BTC floor (~$30K-40K depending on energy costs).

MARKET IS A CROWD — Not mechanical or electronic — huge crowd under imperfect mass psychology. Physics and math help, but decisions MUST account for psychology. This is why fully automatic systems fail. Crowd has moods, panics, euphoria. Technical indicators measure crowd behavior quantitatively. Ask: what is the crowd feeling?

JUST BECAUSE YOU KNOW — Doesn't mean everyone does. The market will go higher than you think, and lower. Most people don't have foresight to look 6 months out. Don't impose your timeline on the market. Be flexible. The market is always right; you are often wrong.

DON'T PERSONALIZE — Market is totally impersonal; doesn't care whether you make money. When trader says 'I wish' or 'I hope,' they engage in destructive thinking. Whenever buying, make sure you're not going to lose money first. Value first, then catalyst, then timing.

MARKETS LOOK BEST AT HIGHS — When setting new highs, that is often the BEST time to sell. To be good trader you must be contrarian. Best money is made at turns, not by catching trends in the middle. But: being contrarian doesn't automatically mean victory. Need RIGHT TIMING and APPROPRIATE SIZE.

VARIANT PERCEPTION — Develop perceptions at variance with general market view. Play those variant perceptions until no longer variant. Perception must be CONTRARIAN — if everyone agrees, edge is gone. When stocks don't act as fundamentals suggest, investigate what the other side knows.

EXPECT THE EXTREME — Don't think in boundaries. Unexpected and impossible happen regularly. Don't draw boundaries from history. 'BTC at $20K couldn't go higher' → $69K. 'BTC at $69K can't go lower' → $15K. Always account for scenarios beyond your imagination.

DON'T TRADE WHEN YOU CAN'T AFFORD TO — Trading with scared money guarantees failure: miss best opportunities, jump out of good positions, take profits too fast, stay with losers. Exceptional traders need confidence AND desire. They come together only occasionally for most.

BEST INDICATORS ARE SIMPLE — Moving averages, breakouts. Complexity doesn't equal edge. Simple trend-following works because it captures persistent tendency of markets to trend. Weiss: best indicators are simple ones. If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it well enough.

RISK MANAGEMENT IS THE SYSTEM — Entry signal is secondary. Focus on: how much to risk per trade, when to increase/decrease size, how to handle correlated positions. This is the real edge. Basso: risk management IS the system. The entry is merely the trigger.

TRADING FOR FREEDOM — Freedom requires discipline, risk tolerance, facility with numbers. Discipline = choosing what you want MOST over what you want NOW. Without discipline, no system works. With discipline, even mediocre system produces profits. Freedom is earned through thousands of disciplined decisions.

MONEY SUPPLY GROWTH DIFFERENTIALS — M2 growth exceeding GDP growth = inflationary, currency bearish. Monitor M2 velocity changes. Quantitative easing expands money supply, diluting currency value. For crypto: expanding global M2 = BTC tailwind. Contracting = headwind.

NEURAL NETWORK CAUTION — Extremely prone to curve fitting. Require massive data sets. Often fail in live trading. Most useful as ONE input in system, not standalone. Use for pattern recognition (classify market regime) rather than direct signal generation. Spectacular backtest results = probably overfit.

TRADING FOR A LIVING vs KILLING — Beginners want to make a killing. Professionals want to make a living. Goal: consistent monthly income, not lottery wins. Small consistent gains compound. $100K at 3% monthly = $42K annual. That's a living. Those who try to make a killing end up killing their account.

PERSONAL INTEGRITY — Your word to yourself matters. Each broken rule makes next one easier to break. Each kept rule strengthens discipline. Write rules down. Post where visible. If you violate, write WHY in journal. Pattern recognition of your rule-breaking is first step to eliminating it.

SELF-SABOTAGE PATTERNS — 1) Not taking valid setups (fear). 2) Moving stops against you (hope). 3) Adding to losers (denial). 4) Taking profits too early (fear of losing gains). 5) Revenge trading (anger). 6) Overtrading (boredom). 7) System hopping (frustration). Write rules BEFORE, follow exactly, review weekly.

THE HOLY GRAIL IS YOU — Most look for mysterious system. Real Holy Grail: understanding yourself — biases, objectives, risk tolerance, time availability, emotional triggers. Different people need different systems based on personality. A system that works for one may fail for another. Self-knowledge comes BEFORE system design.

JUDGMENTAL BIASES — 1) Reliability bias: overvaluing win rates. 2) Lotto bias: seeking huge wins with tiny probability. 3) Representation bias: small samples = whole. 4) Randomness bias: patterns in noise. 5) Conservatism bias: slow to update. 6) Small numbers bias. Awareness is first step to overcoming.

12 STEPS TO SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT — 1) Take inventory. 2) Develop open mind. 3) Determine objectives. 4) Choose timeframe. 5) Study best historical moves. 6) Identify concept. 7) Add stops and costs. 8) Add exits, calculate expectancy. 9) Look for huge reward trades. 10) Optimize with sizing. 11) Determine improvements. 12) Worst-case mental planning.

SIX KEYS TO SUCCESS — 1) Positive expectancy system. 2) Trade consistently. 3) Position sizing to maximize expectancy. 4) Opportunity factor. 5) Transaction costs. 6) Starting capital adequacy. Most focus only on #1. HOLY GRAIL is understanding that #3 determines MOST of success or failure.

OBJECTIVES BEFORE SYSTEM — Objectives determine EVERYTHING: timeframe, markets, risk, sizing, system type. Must know: monthly return target, max drawdown, time available, capital, psychological risk tolerance. Without clear objectives, you'll chase random systems. Write objectives first.

LOSS AVERSION — 30-40% win rate feels like failure even if profitable. Don't look at individual P&L. Monthly only. Most traders quit trend-following during losing streaks, missing the big winners that make the system work. Big trends follow biggest pain. Accept the math.

AMATEUR vs PROFESSIONAL — Amateurs: chase, one attempt, freeze on losses, average down, predict market. Professionals: multiple entries, cut quickly, never average down, react to market. If stopped on false breakout, pros re-enter; amateurs stay out. Pros risk <2%, amateurs want leverage.

FEAR IS STRONGER THAN GREED — Bearish moves are faster and sharper than bullish. Tops form slowly (distribution), bottoms form quickly (capitulation). Position sizing should reflect this asymmetry. Use tighter stops on short positions. Allow more room on longs.

EMOTIONAL DETACHMENT — Good traders are dedicated but not emotionally married. Accept losses and make decisions intellectually. During losing streaks, take a break — continuing breeds greater losses. Emotions are fine at weddings, NOT in trading. When emotional, you are the signal to STOP.

LOSING BEGETS LOSING — When you start losing, triggers negative psychology and pessimism. During losing streaks: cut back rapidly, stop completely if bad enough. Take 3-4 weeks off to reset. Don't fight back by trading heavier. Biggest losses ALWAYS follow largest profits. After strong run, play smaller.

AFTER DEVASTATING LOSS — Play very small — shrink to 1/5 or 1/10 normal position. Get black ink (any profit) to rebuild rhythm and confidence. It's not how much you make, but getting confidence back. Works consistently. Take time between loss and next decision.
